title: E 8 Radiant Warmer
learningObjectives: Recognise the clinical situations that require a neonatal radiant warmer.
Explain the four mechanisms of neonatal heat loss and how radiant warmers counteract them.
Select and set the appropriate warming mode (pre‑warm, automatic/servo, manual).
Demonstrate safe set‑up, patient preparation, placement, monitoring and removal of a newborn on a radiant warmer.
Apply infection‑prevention and cleaning protocols to maintain device safety.
Identify common clinical and device‑related complications and initiate appropriate corrective actions.
Troubleshoot basic power, heating and probe failures before contacting maintenance.

sections.title: Clinical Problem
learningObjectives: Understand why temperature < 36 °C at birth increases mortality risk.
Identify newborn groups for which radiant warming is indicated.

sections.title: Assessment
learningObjectives: Describe the four mechanisms of neonatal heat loss (evaporation, radiation, conduction, convection).
Know the normal axillary temperature range (36.5 °C‑37.5 °C).
Differentiate the three warming modes (pre‑warm, automatic/servo, manual).

subsections.title: Heat‑loss mechanisms & warming modes
subsections.content: Summarises evaporation, radiation, conduction and convection; explains how radiant warmers provide focused radiative heat and outlines pre‑warm, automatic/servo and manual settings.

sections.title: Management
learningObjectives: Set‑up the warmer safely (power, temperature probe, heating mode).
Prepare the newborn and required consumables before positioning.
Place the infant correctly and secure the temperature probe.
Monitor temperature and alarms according to the selected mode.
Remove the infant safely and disinfect the device.

subsections.title: Setting up for a patient
subsections.content: Plug power, select pre‑warm or 25 % manual, connect temperature probe and verify heat from the overhead elements.

subsections.title: Preparing a patient
subsections.content: Gather tape, gauze, 70 % alcohol; explain procedure to guardians; perform hand‑washing; ensure the baby is dry and wearing a hat.

subsections.title: Starting a patient
subsections.content: Pre‑warm the cot, switch to servo/automatic mode, position infant centrally, clean and place the temperature probe over the liver (or flank for prone babies) and secure it.

subsections.title: Caring for a patient
subsections.content: Monitor temperature 5 min after start then every 4 h (servo) or 30 min (manual); respond to temperature, probe, power and system alarms.

subsections.title: Removing a patient
subsections.content: Disconnect tape, disinfect probe site and probe, turn off and unplug the warmer, check infant’s temperature after 30 min, and disinfect the cot before reuse.

sections.title: Infection Prevention
learningObjectives: Apply hand‑ hygiene before and after patient contact.
Ensure all consumables (probes, tape, gauze) are clean or single‑use.
Follow post‑use disinfection procedures for probe, control panel, mattress and cot.

subsections.title: General infection prevention
subsections.content: Hand washing, use of new/clean consumables, proper storage of cables, and dedicated probe per infant.

subsections.title: Disinfection after use
subsections.content: Turn off and cool the unit, wipe probe, panel, power button, mattress and cot with gauze and 70 % alcohol or diluted chlorine.

sections.title: Complications
learningObjectives: Identify clinical complications (hypothermia, hyperthermia, pressure sores, falls, infection).
Recognise device‑related complications (probe mis‑management, alarm mis‑setting, fire risk).

subsections.title: Clinical complications
subsections.content: Discusses hypothermia, hyperthermia, pressure sores, falls and infection risks linked to improper use.

subsections.title: Device complications
subsections.content: Covers overheating from probe loss, improper alarm thresholds, and fire hazards from linen placement.

sections.title: Care & Maintenance
learningObjectives: Understand power requirements and the need for dedicated mains sockets.
Place the warmer correctly in the ward to minimise drafts.
Perform weekly preventive checks of heating elements, probe and power‑loss alarm.

subsections.title: Power source
subsections.content: Warmers use mains power; plug into a dedicated socket with surge protection; not suitable for low‑power backup systems.

subsections.title: Ward location
subsections.content: Place against a wall, control panel facing centre of room, keep away from windows and drafts.

subsections.title: User preventive maintenance
subsections.content: Weekly test of heating elements & probe by feeling heat and checking temperature rise; test power‑loss alarm by disconnecting mains.

sections.title: Troubleshooting & Repair
learningObjectives: Diagnose why the unit will not power on.
Identify reasons for lack of heat despite power.
Resolve temperature‑probe reading failures.

subsections.title: Problem 1 – Unit does not turn on
subsections.content: Check power cable connection, power switch; replace cable if needed; contact maintenance if still off.

subsections.title: Problem 2 – Unit powers on but does not heat
subsections.content: Verify heating setting is > 0 % (manual) or correct mode selected; replace cable if needed; contact maintenance if heating still absent.

subsections.title: Problem 3 – Probe not reading temperature
subsections.content: Hold probe in hand and watch for temperature change; replace probe if no change or alarm persists; contact maintenance.
